titleStmt.title: Bound states of a quartic and sextic inverse-powerlaw potential for all angular momenta

affiliation.orgName: Department of Physics and Physical Oceanography
Mathematical Modeling and Numerical Simulation Laboratory

analytic.title: Bound states of a quartic and sextic inverse-powerlaw potential for all angular momenta

application.desc: GROBID - A machine learning software for extracting information from scholarly documents

div.p: We use the tridiagonal representation approach to solve the radial SchrГ¶dinger equation for an inverse power-law potential of a combined quartic and sextic degrees and for all angular momenta. The amplitude of the quartic singularity is larger than that of the sextic but the signs are negative and positive, respectively. It turns out that the system has a finite number of bound states, which is determined by the larger ratio of the two singularity amplitudes. The solution is written as a finite series of square integrable functions written in terms of the Bessel polynomial.

div.head: Introduction
div.p: Power-law potential models have significant applications in various areas of physics, including atomic and molecular physics, particle physics, and gravity physics [1][2][3][4][5][6][7][8][9]. However, most of these models are exactly solvable if they are less singular than the inverse cube, which is relevant to applications such as electric quadrupole coupling. In this work, we consider the following inverse power-law radial effective potential (including the orbital term) in 3D with spherical symmetry and with quartic and sextic singularities: ( 1) 1 () 22 ba Vr r r r
div.formula: пЂ« пЂ« пЃЊ пЂЅ пЂ­ пЂ« ,(1)
div.p: where is the angular momentum quantum number and пЃЊ is a dimensionless real parameter. The other length parameters, a and b, are real such that ba пЂѕ . From this point forward, we set 0 пЃЊпЂЅ . This potential is highly singular with a finite number of bound states. In the published literature, such potentials are treated only formally. Exact solutions of the SchrГ¶dinger equation with this potential for all energies and all angular momenta, which is a highly non-trivial task, has never been reported. In section 2.6 of Ref. [10], Dong studied potential (1) in D dimensions with the extra oscillator term пЃ· and obtained an energy spectrum proportional to пЃ· and independent of . Therefore, that study gives only a zero energy S-wave solution for potential (1), which is in contradiction to our findings. The Author repeated the same study in 2D with cylindrical symmetry but also found zero energy S-wave solutions [11]. A similar earlier study by Dong and Ma in [12] also resulted in zero energy solutions. Nonetheless, in this work we were able to obtain a solution (energy spectrum and wavefunction) for this potential using the пЂ­2пЂ­ "tridiagonal representation approach" (TRA) (for details of the TRA, one may consult [13] and references therein). The solution of the current problem is written as a finite series of square integrable function written in terms of the Bessel polynomial. The expansion coefficients are orthogonal polynomials in the argument пЂЁ пЂ©

div.head: пЂ«
div.p: with parameters that depend on the energy and пЂЁ пЂ© 2 ba . These polynomials are new and contain all physical information about the system. In sections 2 and 3, we formulate the problem and solve it within the TRA then present the results in sections 4 and 5.

div.head: TRA formulation of the problem
div.p: In the atomic units 1 m пЂЅпЂЅ , the time-independent radial SchrГ¶dinger equation for the inverse power-law potential (1) with 0 пЃЊпЂЅ reads as follows
div.formula: 2 2 4 2 2 4 6 1 ( 1) 1 ( ) 0 2 2 2 d b a Er dr r r r пЃ№ пѓ©пѓ№ пЂ« пЂ­ пЂ« пЂ­ пЂ« пЂ­ пЂЅ пѓЄпѓє пѓ«пѓ» , (2
div.p: where E is the energy and () r пЃ№ is the corresponding radial wavefunction. Now, we employ the tools of the TRA in the formulation and solution of this problem (see [13] and references therein). We start by expanding the wavefunction in a series as ( ) ( )
div.formula: nn n r f x пЃ№пЃ¦ пЂЅ пѓҐ
div.p: , where
div.formula: пЃ» пЃЅ () n x
div.p: пЃ¦ is a set of square integrable functions that produce a tridiagonal matrix representation for the wave operator (2). We choose the dimensionless variable x as
div.formula: пЂЁ пЂ© 2 x r a пЂЅ
div.p: . In terms of this variable, the wave equation (2) becomes
div.formula: пЂЁ пЂ© 22 2 2 2 2 2 1 1 4 ( ) ( ) 1 ( ) 0 2 4 2 2 d x d b a r x x r a x dx dx x x пЃҐ пЃ№пЃ№ пѓ©пѓ№ пЂ­ пЂЅ пЂ« пЂ­ пЂ« пЂ­ пЂ« пЂ« пЂЅ пѓЄпѓє пѓ«пѓ» . (3
div.p: where 2 aE пЃҐ пЂЅ . A proper square-integrable basis that could support a tridiagonal matrix representation for the wave operator has the following elements 12 ( ) ( )
div.formula: x n n n x A x e Y x пЃЎпЃ­ пЃ¦ пЂ­ пЂЅ ,(4) where (
n
div.p: Yx пЃ­ is the Bessel polynomial on the positive real line whose relevant properties are given in the Appendix. The normalization constant is conveniently chosen using the orthogonality relation (A3) as (2 2 1) ! ( 2)
div.formula: n A n n n пЃ­пЃ­ пЂЅ пЂ­ пЂ« пЂ« пЃ‡ пЂ­ пЂ­
div.p: . The degree of the polynomial is limited by the negative parameter пЃ­ where 0,1,2,.., nN пЂЅ
with N being the largest integer less than
div.formula: 1 2 1 2 4 2( ) ( ) 2 2 1 2 ( ) 2 x n nn A ba x x e n x Y x ax пЃ­ пЃ­ пЃ­ пЃ¦ пЃ­ пЃҐ пЂ­ пЂ­ пѓ©пѓ№ пЂ­ пЂ« пЂЅ пЂ« пЂ« пЂ­ пЂ« пЂ« пЂ« пѓЄпѓє пѓ«пѓ» . (5
div.p: The TRA dictates that this action takes the following form
div.formula: пЃ› пЃќ 1 1 1 ( ) ( ) ( ) ( ) ( ) n n n n n n n x x u x s x t x пЃ¦ пЃІ пЃ¦ пЃ¦ пЃ¦ пЂ­ пЂ­ пЂ« пЂЅ пЂ« пЂ« ,(6)
div.p: where with constant factors. Hence, the three-term recursion relation (A2) dictates that the terms inside the square brackets is permitted to be only linear in x. Thus, the term proportional to 1 x пЂ­ must vanish and we should choose the basis parameters пЃ­ as follows
div.formula: пЂЁ пЂ© 2 2 ba пЃ­ пЂЅпЂ­ . (7
div.p: This is reassuring since the basis parameter пЃ­ is required to be negative. In fact, it should be less than 1 2 пЂ­ , which is consistent with the physical requirement that ba пЂѕ . Moreover, since N is the largest integer less than
пЃ­ пЂ­пЂ­, then the maximum number of bound states that could be obtained by our TRA solution is
div.formula: пЂЁ пЂ© 2 max 11 22 1 N b a пѓЄпѓє пЂ« пЂЅ пЂ« пѓ«пѓ» ,(8)
div.p: which is independent of the angular momentum. However, the actual size of the bound states spectrum does depend on the angular momentum as will be confirmed by our results below. In fact, physically we expect that the number of bound states to decrease with increasing where the disappearing bound states turn into resonances [14]. Therefore, the maximum number of bound states given by Eq. ( 8) corresponds to 0 пЂЅ . In the following section, we present the TRA solution of the problem which is written in terms of a new polynomial defined in [15] by its three-term recursion relation and initial values.

div.head: TRA solution of the problem
div.p: With the basis parameters пЃ­ given by (7), the action of the wave operator on the basis elements (4) reduces to the following
div.formula: пЂЁ пЂ© пЂЁ пЂ© 1 4 2 2 12 2 1 2 ( ) 2 2 1 2 ( ) 2 x n n n A x x e n x Y x a пЃ­ пЃ­ пЃ¦ пЃ­ пЃҐ пЂ­ пЂ­ пЂ­ пѓ©пѓ№ пЂЅ пЂ« пЂ« пЂ­ пЂ« пЂ« пѓЄпѓє пѓ«пѓ» . (9

пЃ­пЃҐ пЃ­ пЃ­пЃ­ пЃ­пЃ­ пЃҐ пЃ­ пЃ­ пЃ­ пЃ­ пЃ­ пЃ­ пЂ«пЂ­ пѓ©пѓ№ пЂ« пЂЅ пЂ« пЂ« пЂ­ пѓЄпѓє пЂ« пЂ« пЂ« пѓ«пѓ» пѓ© пѓ№ пЂ­ пЂ« пЂ« пЂ« пЂ­ пЂ« пЂ«пЂ« пѓЄ пѓє пЂ« пЂ« пЂ« пЂ« пЂ« пЂ« пЂ« пЂ« пЂ­ пЂ« пЂ« пѓ« пѓ»(10)
div.p: where we have written

div.head: пЂ«
div.p: with parameters that depend on the energy пЃҐ and пЃ­.

div.head: Comparing this recursion relation to that of the polynomial ( ; )
div.p: n Bz пЃ­ пЃ§ defined in [15] by its three- term recursion relation, which is shown here in the Appendix as (A10), we conclude that ( ; )
div.formula: n n n P A B z пЃ­ пЃ§ пЂЅ with 8 пЃ§пЃҐ пЂЅ , пЂЁ пЂ©пЂЁ пЂ© 2 1 2 2 z пЃҐ пЂЅпЂ« . (11
div.p: Finally, the kth bound state wavefunction for this power-law potential reads
div.formula: пЂЁ пЂ© 3 22 4 2 2 2 2 2 2 0 0 ( ) ( ) ( ; ) ( ) N ar k k n n n n r f E r a e A B z Y r a пЃ­ пЃ­пЃ­ пЃ№пЃ§ пЂ« пЂ­ пЂЅ пЂЅ пѓҐ . (12
div.p: For a given set of physical parameters пЃ» пЃЅ

,, ab and bound state energy k E , the basis parameter пЃ­ is given by ( 7) whereas z and пЃ§ are given by (11). All properties of the system (e.g., energy spectrum of the bound states, density of states, etc.) are obtained from the properties of the polynomial ( ; ) n Bz пЃ­ пЃ§ (e.g., its weight function, generating function, asymptotics, zeros, etc.). For example, the energy spectrum could easily be obtained from the spectrum formula of this polynomial. Unfortunately, the analytic properties of ( ; )
div.formula: n Bz пЃ­ пЃ§ are not yet known.
div.p: It remains an open problem in orthogonal polynomials along with other similar problems (for an expose of these problems, one may consult [16] and references therein). Therefore, we are forced to resort to numerical means to calculate the physical properties of the system. The most important property needed for our system is the energy spectrum пЃ» пЃЅ k E because then the corresponding wavefunction (12) will be fully determined. This will be done in the following section where we obtain the bound states energy spectrum for a given set of physical parameters пЃ» пЃЅ ,, ab .

div.head: The energy spectrum
div.p: To find the energy spectrum numerically in the absence of the analytic properties of the TRA polynomial ( ; ) n Bz пЃ­ пЃ§ , we might be tempted to use the recursion relation (10) written as an eigenvalue equation q
div.formula: P T P пЂЅ where пЂЁ пЂ© 2 1 2
div.p: q пЂЅпЂ« and T is the symmetric tridiagonal matrix
div.formula: , , 1 , 1 , 1 n m n n m n n m n n m T A B B пЃ¤ пЃ¤ пЃ¤ пЂ­ пЂ« пЂ­ пЂЅ пЂ« пЂ« with пЂЁ пЂ© 2 2 2 1 ( )( 1) n An nn пЃҐпЃ­ пЃ­ пЃ­пЃ­ пЂ­ пЂЅ пЂ« пЂ« пЂ« пЂ« пЂ« пЂ« ,(13a)
n nn B n n n пЃҐпЃ­ пЃ­ пЃ­ пЃ­ пЂ­ пЂ« пЂ« пЂ« пЂЅ пЂ« пЂ« пЂ« пЂ« пЂ« пЂ« . (13b

div.head: пЂ­5пЂ­
div.p: However, the problem in finding the energy spectrum this way is that the matrix elements of T do depend on the energy itself that we are trying to find. Of course, out of all energies that enter in the construction of T, the discrete energies that belong to the spectrum are only those that produce the eigenvalue
div.formula: пЂЁ пЂ© 2 1 2
div.p: q пЂЅпЂ« for a given angular momentum . Therefore, we choose an alternative numerical technique to accomplish this task. It goes as follows: The action of the Hamiltonian operator on the basis elements,
div.formula: п‚Ґ пЂ­ пЂЅ пѓІ пЂЁ пЂ© 1 1 2 0 2 ... x dx п‚Ґ пЂ­пЂ­ пѓІ . The result is an ( 1) ( 1) NN пЂ« п‚ґ пЂ« diagonal matrix with the following elements пЂЁ пЂ© пЂЁ пЂ© 2 2 , 2 1 2 1 2 2 1 4 n m n m Hn a пЃ¦ пЃ¦ пЃ­ пЃ¤ пѓ©пѓ№ пЂЅ пЂ« пЂ­ пЂ« пЂ« пѓЄпѓє пѓ«пѓ» ,(14)
div.p: where , 0,1,2,.., n m N пЂЅ . These diagonal elements would have been identified as the energy eigenvalues had the basis set (4) been orthogonal; but it is not. The energy spectrum is obtained by solving the generalized eigenvalue equation HE пЃ№пЃ№ пЂЅпЃ— , where пЃ— is the basis overlap matrix whose elements are
div.formula: ,, n m n m n m пЃ¦ пЃ¦ пЃ¤ пЃ— пЂЅ п‚№ .
div.p: Using the orthogonality (A3) and the recursion relation (A2) together with the integration measure noted above, we obtain пЃ— as the following
div.formula: ( 1) ( 1) NN пЂ« п‚ґ пЂ« tridiagonal symmetric matrix пЂЁ пЂ© , ,1 , 1 , 1 1 4
n m n n m n n m n n m A B B пЃ¤ пЃ¤ пЃ¤ пЂ­ пЂ« пЂ­ пЃ— пЂЅ пЂ« пЂ« . (15
div.p: where n A and n B are the factors that multiply пЃҐ in n A and n B of Eq. ( 13), respectively. Now, with the matrices H and пЃ— being determined, we can obtain the energy spectrum of the bound states as the negative subset of the 1 N пЂ« eigenvalues of the generalized eigenvalue equation HE пЃ№пЃ№ пЂЅпЃ— . These are shown in Table 1 for a given set of physical parameters пЃ» пЃЅ , ab and several values of the angular momentum. Since this energy spectrum is obtained numerically using finite matrices whose size, which could be very small, is dictated by the physical parameters a and b, then it would be desirable to obtain an independent verification of these results using other numerical means. This is carried out in the following section where we obtain the energy spectrum by two different numerical methods.

div.head: Results and discussion
div.p: In the first method, we select an energy independent complete square integrable basis in which the matrix representation of the Hamiltonian is tridiagonal and symmetric. Then, we diagonalize a finite submatrix of this Hamiltonian for a given set of physical parameters пЃ» пЃЅ

,, ab and obtain the corresponding energy spectrum as the negative eigenvalues of this finite submatrix. We will also demonstrate that the spectrum converges rapidly with an increase in the size of the submatrix. Moreover, we compare these results with those obtained by higher order finite difference schemes for solving the eigenvalue problem [19].

div.head: пЂ­6пЂ­
div.p: For the complete square integrable basis, we choose the "Laguerre basis" whose elements read as follows пЃЎ пЂЅ , the action of the Hamiltonian operator, ()
div.formula: d dr H V r пЂЅ пЂ­ пЂ« , on the basis (16) becomes пЂЁ пЂ© пЂЁ пЂ© пЂЁ пЂ© пЃ» пЃЅ 24 2 2 2 1 2 ( ) 2 1 1 ( ) y n n n H y C y e n b a y L y пЃЎ пЃ® пЃЈ пЃ¬ пЃ® пЃ¬ пЃ¬ пЂ«пЂ­ пѓ©пѓ№ пЂЅ пЂ« пЂ« пЂ­ пЂ« пЂ­ пѓ«пѓ» , (17
div.p: where we have used пЂЁ пЂ©
div.formula: пЃ® пЃ® пѓ©пѓ№ пЂ« пЂ« пЂ­ пЂ« пЂЅ пѓЄпѓє пѓ«пѓ» . (18
div.p: Now, we use the recursion relation of the Laguerre polynomials, ( ) (2 1) ( )
div.formula: nn y L y n L y пЃ®пЃ® пЃ® пЂЅ пЂ« пЂ« пЂ­ 11 ( ) ( ) ( 1) ( ) nn n L y n L y пЃ®пЃ® пЃ® пЂ­пЂ« пЂ« пЂ­ пЂ«
div.p: for the last term inside the curly brackets in (17). The result is as follows
div.formula: пЂЁ пЂ© пЂЁ пЂ© пЂЁ пЂ© пЃ» пЃЅ пЂЁ пЂ© пЂЁ пЂ© пЂЁ пЂ©пЂЁ пЂ© пЃ» пЃЅ 2 42 2 2 4 2 11 ( ) 1 2 1 2 ( ) 2 1 ( ) 1 1 ( ) 2 n n nn H y y a n b y y a n n y n n y пЃ¬ пЃЈ пЃ¬ пЃ® пЃ¬ пЃЈ пЃ¬ пЃ¬ пЃ® пЃЈ пЃ® пЃЈ пЂ­пЂ« пѓ©пѓ№ пЂЅ пЂ« пЂ« пЂ« пЂ­ пѓ«пѓ» пѓ©пѓ№ пЂ­ пЂ­ пЂ« пЂ« пЂ« пЂ« пЂ« пѓ«пѓ»

пЂЁ пЂ© пЂЁ пЂ© пЂЁ пЂ© пЃ» пЃЅ пЂЁ пЂ© пЂЁ пЂ© пЂЁ пЂ©пЂЁ пЂ© пЃ» пЃЅ 2 42 , 2 4 , 1 , 1 3 2 3 1 22 1 2 2 2 11 2 n m n m n m n m H a n b a n n n n пЃ¬ пЃЈ пЃЈ пЃ¬ пЃ¬ пЃ¤ пЃ¬ пЃ¬ пЃ¤ пЃ¤ пЂ«пЂ­ пѓ©пѓ№ пЂЅ пЂ« пЂ« пЂ« пЂ­ пѓ«пѓ» пѓ©пѓ№ пЂ­ пЂ­ пЂ« пЂ« пЂ« пЂ« пЂ« пЂ« пѓ«пѓ» (20)
div.p: Therefore, the energy spectrum пЃ» пЃЅ 0 K k k E пЂЅ is obtained as the 1 K пЂ« negative eigenvalues of the following generalized eigenvalue equation
div.formula: k k k E пЃ№пЃ№ пЂЅпЃ— ,(21)
div.p: where is the Hamiltonian matrix whose elements are given by (20) and пЃ— is the overlap matrix of the basis (16)
div.formula: пЃ® пЃ® пЃ® пЃЈ пЃЈ пЃ¬ пЃЈ пЃЈ п‚Ґп‚Ґ пЂ­пЂ­ пЃ— пЂЅ пЂЅ пЂЅ пѓІпѓІ . (22
div.p: This integral could be evaluate numerically using Gauss quadrature integral approximation associated with the Laguerre polynomials (see, for example, Appendix B in [20]). Rigorously, the integrability of (22) dictates that the exponent 2 пЃ® пЂ­ of y must be greater than пЂ­1. Thus, with 1 2 пЃ® пЂЅпЂ« , we expect integration difficulty only for 0 пЂЅ . However, numerically we can improve the accuracy of the result of integration by increasing the size of the basis ( 16) and choosing a proper value of the scale parameter пЃ¬. For a given choice of the physical parameters пЃ» пЃЅ , ab and several angular momenta, Table 2 gives the complete bound states energies. One should note that the overlap matrix пЃ— does not depend on the non-physical computational scale parameter пЃ¬ but the Hamiltonian matrix does. However, this dependence is a numerical artifact in the sense that the computed energy eigenvalues пЃ» пЃЅ 0 K k k E пЂЅ will be independent of пЃ¬ if the size of the matrices becomes very large. For finite size matrices, though, we search for a range of values of пЃ¬ where the eigenvalues do not change (within the desired accuracy) as we vary пЃ¬ within this range. This range is called the "plateau of stability" for пЃ¬ whose width increases with the size of the matrices. Theoretically, the width of the plateau goes to infinity as the size of the matrices go to infinity. Our choice of пЃ¬ for all results in this work is picked up from the middle of the plateau. Table 3 shows good convergence of the energy spectrum as the size of the matrices and пЃ— increases.
For the second method, which is based on the finite difference scheme [19], we start by applying a change of variable
div.formula: пЃ° пЃ° пЃ° пЃґпЃ° пЃ°пЃ№ пЃ°пЃґ пѓ¬пѓј пЂ­ пЂ« пЂ« пЂ­ пЂЅ пѓ­пѓЅ пѓ®пѓѕ , (23
div.p: where пЃґ is some real positive parameter of inverse length dimension to be fixed later and
div.formula: пЂЁ пЂ© пЂЁ пЂ© пЂЁ пЂ© пЂЁ пЂ© 2 2 4 2 2 4 2 2 2 2 2( ) ( ) ( ) 1 tan tan tan ba Vs s s s пЃ° пЃ° пЃ° пЃґ пЃґ пЃґ пѓ©пѓ№ пѓЄпѓє пЂЅ пЂ« пЂ­ пЂ« пѓЄпѓє пѓ«пѓ» , (24
div.p: Next, we discretize the finite interval пЃ› пЃќ
div.formula: k i j j j k k j i j k j s k i t j j t j ik d i k k M k ds i M k M t h Mk пЃ№ пЃ№ пЃ№ пЃ¤ пЃ№ пЃ¤ пЃ¤ пЂЅ пЂ«пЂ­ пЂЅ пЂ­пЂ« пЂЅ пЂЅпЂ­ пЂЅпЂ« пѓ¬ пѓЇ пѓЇ пѓЇ пѓЇ п‚» пЂ«пЂ­ пѓ­ пѓЇ пѓЇ пЂ­ пЂЅпЂ­ пѓЇ пѓЇ пЂ«пЂЅ пЂ« пѓ® пѓҐ пѓҐ пѓҐ (25) пЂ­8пЂ­ 21 , ,2 0 2 , ,2 2 0 2 ,1 0 2 1 ,2 2 , 1, 2,3,..., 1 , , 1,..., 1 , 2 ,..., 1 2 & 1 i k i j j j k k j i j k j s k k j j t j ik d i k k M k ds iM h kt Mk M пЃ№ пЃ№ пЃ№ пЃ№ пЃ¤ пЃ¤ пЃ¤ пЂ« пЂЅ пЂ«пЂ­ пЂЅ пЂ« пЂ«пЂ­ пЂЅ пЂЅпЂ­ пЂЅ пЂ« пЂ« пЂ­ пЂЅ пЂ« пЂ­ пѓ¬ пѓЇ пѓЇ пѓЇ пѓЇ п‚» пѓ­ пѓЇ пѓЇ пЂ­ пѓЇ пЂЅ пЂ« пѓЇ пѓ® пѓҐ пѓҐ пѓҐ (26) where пЃ» пЃЅ ,, i j l пЃ¤ with 1, 2 l пЂЅ
div.p: are the weights which are determined by requiring the formula to have maximum order of consistency (see [19] for more details), and
div.formula: пЂЁ пЂ© 21 О”О” M J A B CI E пЃ™ пЂЅ пЂ« пЂ« пЃ™ пЂЅ пЃ™ ,(27)
div.p: where A , B and C are diagonal matrices of size M which contain the coefficients in Eq. ( 23).
div.formula: О” l with 1, 2 l пЂЅ
div.p: are real square matrices of size M which contain the coefficients ,,
div.formula: l i j l h пЃ¤ . M I is the MM п‚ґ identity matrix and пЂЁ пЂ© 12 , , , T M пЃ№ пЃ№ пЃ№ пЃ™ пЂЅ п‚ј
div.p: . The parameter пЃґ is chosen as
div.formula: пЂЁ пЂ© 0.7 0.6 j пЃґ пЂ­ пЂЅ
div.p: , where 1,..., jM пЂЅ is the eigenvalue index [19]. Therefore, the complete solutions of the SchrГ¶dinger equation ( 2) are approximated by the eigenvalues and the corresponding eigenvectors of the matrix J . In Table 4, we have computed the eigenvalues of J for the given choice of parameters using "Matlab" which is in good agreement with the results obtained by the Hamiltonian diagonalization method in the Laguerre basis (Table 2). The discrepancy between the results in Tables 1 and those in Table 2 or 4 is expected due to the finite size basis used in producing Table 1 as at the end of Sec. 4.

Figure 1 is a plot of the energy spectrum for the lowest states as a function of the angular momentum. In an attempt to discover the energy spectrum formula, we used the linfit built-in function in Mathcad that fits data points to a properly chosen function suggested by the shapes of the curves in Figure 1. Accordingly, we obtained the following possible energy spectrum formula
div.formula: 2 20 ( , ) ( ) ( ) E n C n C n пЂЅпЂ­ ,(28)
div.p: where the two functions 0 () Cn and 2 () Cn are positive that also depend on the physical parameters пЃ» пЃЅ , ab . Moreover, the figure suggests that 2 ()
div.formula: d dn Cn is negative. Figure 2 is a plot
div.p: of the energy spectrum пЃ» пЃЅ n E as a function of n for several angular momenta. Figure 3 is a plot of the bound states wavefunctions corresponding to the 3 пЂЅ column in Table 2.

div.head: Conclusion
div.p: In this work, we obtained a finite exact series solution of the SchrГ¶dinger equation for the bound states of a radial inverse power-law potential with quartic and sextic singularities. The solution series is written in terms of square integrable functions using the Bessel polynomial. The пЂ­9пЂ­ expansion coefficients are orthogonal polynomials in the energy, angular momentum and potential parameters. We used the tridiagonal representation approach as the method of solution and verified our results numerically via two independent computational methods. We expect that the solution obtained here will have fruitful applications in areas where inverse power-law potential models are relevant such as in molecular physics and high energy physics.

div.head: Appendix: Bessel polynomial on the real line
div.p: The Bessel polynomial on the positive real line is defined in terms of the hypergeometric or confluent hypergeometric functions as follows (see section 9.13 of the book by Koekoek et. al [21] but make the replacement 2 xx and
div.formula: 2 a пЃ­ ) пЂЁ пЂ© пЂЁ пЂ© пЂЁ пЂ© 2 0 1 1 , 2 1 2( ) ( ) 2 1 1 n n n nn n n Y x F x n x F x пЃ­ пЃ­ пЃ­ пЃ­ пЂ­ пЂ« пЂ« пЂ­ пЃџпЃџ пЂ­пЂ« пЂЅ пЂ­ пЂЅ пЂ« пЂ« , (A1
пЃ­ пЃ­пЃ­ пЃ­ пЃ­ пЃ­ пЃ­ пЃ­ пЂ­пЂ« пЂ­ пЂЅ пЂ« пЂ« пЂ« пЂ«пЂ« пЂ­пЂ« пЂ« пЂ« пЂ« пЂ« пЂ« пЂ« пЂ« (A2)
div.p: Note that the constraints on пЃ­ and on the maximum polynomial degree make this recursion definite (i.e., the signs of the two recursion coefficients multiplying 1 () n Yx пЃ­ п‚± are the same). Otherwise, these polynomials could not be defined on the real line but on the unit circle in the complex plane. The orthogonality relation reads as follows
div.formula: 21 0 ! ( 2 ) ( ) ( ) 2 2 1 x n m nm nn x e Y x Y x dx n пЃ­ пЃ­ пЃ­ пЃ­ пЃ¤ пЃ­ п‚Ґ пЂ­ пЃ‡ пЂ­ пЂ­ пЂЅпЂ­ пЂ«пЂ« пѓІ . (A3)
div.p: The differential equation is
div.formula: пЂЁ пЂ© пЂЁ пЂ© 2 2 2 1 2 1 2 1 ( ) 0 n dd x x n n Y x dx dx пЃ­ пЃ­пЃ­ пѓ¬пѓј пѓ©пѓ№ пЂ« пЂ« пЂ« пЂ­ пЂ« пЂ« пЂЅ пѓ­пѓЅ пѓ«пѓ» пѓ®пѓѕ . (A4
div.p: The forward and backward shift differential relations read as follows
div.formula: пЂЁ пЂ© 1 1 ( ) 2 1 ( ) nn d Y x n n Y x dx пЃ­пЃ­ пЃ­ пЂ« пЂ­ пЂЅ пЂ« пЂ« . (A5) пЂЁ пЂ© 2 1 1 ( ) 2 1 ( ) ( ) n n n d x Y x x Y x Y x dx пЃ­ пЃ­ пЃ­ пЃ­ пЂ­ пЂ« пЂЅ пЂ­ пЂ« пЂ« . (A6
div.p: We can write
div.formula: x п‚і пЂ­10пЂ­ 1 1 11 ( 1)( 2 ) 2 ( ) ( ) ( )( 1) ( 1) ( 2 )( 2 1) ( ) ( ) ( )(2 2 1) ( 1)(2 2 1) nn nn nn Y x Y x nn n n n n Y x Y x n n n n пЃ­пЃ­ пЃ­пЃ­ пЃ­ пЃ­пЃ­ пЃ­пЃ­ пЃ­ пЃ­ пЃ­ пЃ­ пЂ­ пЂ« пЂ­пЂ« пЂ«пЂ« пЂЅ пЂ« пЂ« пЂ« пЂ« пЂ« пЂ« пЂ« пЂ«пЂ« пЂ« пЂ« пЂ« пЂ« пЂ« пЂ« пЂ« (A7)
div.p: Using this identity and the recursion relation (A2), we can rewrite the backward shift differential relation as follows
div.formula: ( ) ( ) ( ) ( )( 1) ( )(2 2 1) ( 1)(2 2 1) n n n n d x Y x n n dx Y x Y x Y x n n n n n n пЃ­ пЃ­ пЃ­ пЃ­ пЃ­ пЃ­ пЃ­ пЃ­ пЃ­ пЃ­ пЃ­ пЂ­пЂ« пЂЅ пЂ« пЂ« п‚ґ пѓ©пѓ№ пЂ­ пЂ« пЂ« пѓЄпѓє пЂ« пЂ« пЂ« пЂ« пЂ« пЂ« пЂ« пЂ« пЂ« пЂ« пѓ«пѓ» (A8) The generating function is пЂЁ пЂ© 2 2 0 2 ( ) 1 1 4 exp 2 (1 1 4 ) ! 14 n n n t Y x xt t xt n xt пЃ­ пЃ­ пЃ­ п‚Ґ пЂ­ пЂЅ пѓ©пѓ№ пЂЅ пЂ« пЂ­ пЂ« пЂ­ пѓ«пѓ» пЂ­ пѓҐ . (A9
div.p: The polynomial ( ; ) n Bz пЃ­ пЃ§ is defined in [15] by its three-term recursion relation Eq. ( 16) therein, which reads
div.formula: nn nn z B z n B z nn nn B z B z n n n n пЃ­пЃ­ пЃ­пЃ­ пЃ­ пЃ§ пЃ§ пЃ­ пЃ§ пЃ­пЃ­ пЃ­ пЃ§пЃ§ пЃ­ пЃ­ пЃ­ пЃ­ пЂ­пЂ« пѓ©пѓ№ пЂ­ пЂЅ пЂ« пЂ« пЂ« пѓЄпѓє пЂ« пЂ« пЂ« пѓ«пѓ» пЂ«пЂ« пЂ« пЂ« пЂ« пЂ« пЂ« пЂ« пЂ« (A10)
div.p: where 0 ( ; ) 1 Bz пЃ­ пЃ§ пЂЅ and 1 ( ; ) : 0
div.formula: Bz пЃ­ пЃ§ пЂ­ пЂЅ .
div.p: Tables Caption:

figure.head: nH
figure.figDesc: пЃ¦ , is obtained from (9) by setting 0 пЃҐ пЂЅ . Subsequently, the Hamiltonian matrix elements mn H пЃ¦пЃ¦ are obtained from that using the orthogonality relation (A3) together with the integration measure
polynomial with пЃ¬ being a positive scale parameter of inverse length dimension and 1пЃ® пЂѕпЂ­ . The normalization constant is chosen as
along with the above approximation schemes, we transform Eq. (3) to the following eigenvalue problem,

figure.head: Figures Caption:Fig. 1 :Fig. 2 :Fig. 3 :
figure.figDesc: Figures Caption:Fig. 1: The bound states energy spectrum (in atomic units) as a function of the angular momentum. The energy levels (0,1,2,..,10) n пЂЅ correspond to the curves from bottom to top. The values of the physical parameters are 2 a пЂЅ and 15 b пЂЅ . Fig. 2: The bound states energy spectrum (in atomic units) as a function of the level quantum number n. The angular momenta (0,10,20,30,40,50) пЂЅ correspond to the curves from bottom to top. The values of the physical parameters are 2 a пЂЅ and 15 b пЂЅ . Fig. 3: Plots of the un-normalized bound states wavefunctions corresponding to the 3 пЂЅ column in Table2.

figure.head: Table 1 :
figure.figDesc: The complete bound states energy spectrum (in atomic units with an overall negative sign) for several angular momenta.

figure.head: Table 2 :
figure.figDesc: Reproduction of Table1using numerical diagonalization of the Hamiltonian matrix in the basis(16). The Hamiltonian matrix size is 100 100 п‚ґ

figure.figDesc: Reproduction of the first column of Table2for different matrix sizes. Good convergence with increasing matrix size is evident. The physical parameters are

figure.head: Table 4 :
figure.figDesc: Reproduction of Table 1 using the 16 th order (

row.cell: 8 k пЂЅ ) finite difference with grid size

analytic.title: Laser-induced photo-association of ultra-cold sodium atoms

Study of Rb 2 Long-Range States by High-Resolution Photo-association Spectroscopy
Molecular hyperfine structure in the photo-association spectroscopy of laser cooled atoms
Elastic scattering of two 7 Li atoms
Photo-associative spectroscopy of ultra-cold 39K atoms in a high-density vapor-cell magneto-optical trap
Solutions of the Schrodinger equation for an attractive 1/r 6 potential
Highly excited bound-state resonances of short-range inverse power-law potentials
On the Solutions of the SchrГ¶dinger Equation with some Anharmonic Potentials: Wave Function Ansatz
Exact Solutions of the Two-Dimensional Schrodinger Equation with Certain Central Potentials
Exact solutions to the SchrГ¶dinger equation for the potential V(r) = ar 2 + br -4 + cr -6 in two dimensions
Tridiagonal Representation Approach in Quantum Mechanics
Taming the Yukawa potential singularity: improved evaluation of bound states and resonance energies
Bound states and the potential parameter spectrum
Approximation of eigenvalues of Sturm-Liouville problems defined on a semi-infinite domain

monogr.title: The J-matrix method of scattering for inverse-square singular potential with supercritical coupling
Hypergeometric Orthogonal Polynomials and Their q-Analogues
